There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

Asking a patient to rate their confidence in quitting on a scale of 1 to 10, and then asking what would make it one point higher, is a simple way to identify what support or information they feel they are missing.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

step2_rag_chunks: Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.

step7_response: You've been considering that phrase and feeling more confident about using it in tempting situations. What do you think would help you remember to use that phrase when you're in the midst of a stressful or tempting situation, like a party with friends?

turns.user_message: I think if I remind myself why I'm quitting - my asthma, feeling healthier - it might help me stay focused on that goal and use the phrase without thinking twice. Or maybe I could write it down or put a note somewhere as a reminder, like on my fridge or something.
step2_rag_chunks: Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.

Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.
